question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseDataLabel]` (line 27) by injecting a `links.next` value that is an extremely long string (e.g., 100KB), causing `linkNextHeaderValue(linksNext)` to produce an oversized `Link` header that exceeds HTTP header size limits, triggering a 431 error or crashing the response pipeline for transaction pagination?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]` injection (line 37) to set a `retry-after` header with a very large value on a transaction response, causing clients to back off for an extended period and effectively denying them access to transaction data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[requestPathLabel]` (line 33) by injecting a path value that contains special regex characters, causing `headers.path[path]` to match unintended path entries in the config and apply incorrect security headers to transaction responses?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]` injection (line 37) to set a `x-accel-redirect` or `x-sendfile` header, causing the web server or reverse proxy to serve an internal file instead of the transaction response, potentially leaking sensitive server-side files?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseDataLabel]` (line 27) by injecting a `links.next` value containing a `file://` or `ftp://` URI scheme, causing the `Link` header to reference a non-HTTP resource that confuses API clients or triggers unintended protocol handlers when followed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]` injection (line 37) to set a `set-cookie` header on a transaction response, injecting a session cookie into the client's browser that could be used for session fixation or CSRF attacks against the mirror node API?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseDataLabel]` (line 27) by injecting a `links.next` value containing a URL with query parameters that include SQL injection payloads, causing the `Link` header to embed these payloads and potentially triggering SQL injection when the next-page URL is followed by the client?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]` injection (line 37) to set a `content-length` header with an incorrect value, causing HTTP clients to truncate or pad the transaction response body, resulting in clients receiving incomplete or corrupted transaction data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseDataLabel]` (line 27) by injecting a `links.next` value that encodes a path traversal sequence (e.g., `/../admin`), causing the `Link` header to reference an internal admin endpoint and potentially exposing it to external clients following the pagination link?
